steps.text: Peer review the radiochemical method of analysis.
Publish the radiochemical method of analysis.
Include an evaluation of all process components combined in the method validation efforts.
Include the sampling procedures in the validation tests.
Clearly and completely state the measurement-process components validated and the combination of procedures composing a method.
Clearly define the purpose of the method and the intended use of the data.
Well define and clearly describe the method scope and applicability.
Evaluate the uncertainty of each input quantity (method parameter) that may contribute significantly to the total uncertainty when estimating the method uncertainty.
Estimate method bias by measuring materials whose composition is reasonably well known, comparing results to those from at least one alternate method or procedure, or analyzing spiked materials.
Investigate the trueness of the measurement method by comparing the accepted reference value with the level of the results given by the measurement method.
Evaluate method repeatability and reproducibility at the extremes of the quantitation range and at regulatory levels.
Clearly state the repeatability and reproducibility conditions.
Include the effects of laboratory subsampling when appropriate during precision and trueness evaluations.
Compare both the standard deviation of repeated measurements at a given analyte level and the bias estimate to the method uncertainty at that analyte level.
Base uncertainty prediction on the same equations that are used to evaluate the uncertainty for each measurement result.

Consider the standard uncertainties of all input quantities (counting uncertainty, detection efficiency, chemical yields, interferences, decay corrections, sample size, etc.) when calculating the MQC of a method.
Study actual samples of material containing an undetermined amount of the inherent radionuclide to evaluate method selectivity.
Conduct ruggedness testing (e.g., using a fractional factorial experimental design).
Identify critical control points in the documented method.
Initiate and complete an interlaboratory method validation study.
Publish the EPA method for general use.
Include the design and results of the interlaboratory study in the method validation summary report.
Report data from the interlaboratory study in tabular form.
Include a discussion describing the details of, and rationale for, any changes made to the method resulting from the interlaboratory study.
Peer review EPA methods of analysis in accordance with the EPA Science Policy Council’s Peer Review Handbook.

preconditions.text: If the radiochemical method includes procedures for sampling (e.g., methods for radon in air analysis and for volatile radioactive organic compounds in soils and other solid matrices).
Suitable reference materials are available, or the accepted reference value can be established by reference to another measurement method or by preparation of a known sample.
Adequate representations of matrix blanks cannot be obtained or prepared.
Prior to publication of EPA methods of analysis

constraints.text: Refer to the most current version of the EPA Science Policy Council’s Peer Review Handbook for both internal and external peer review processes.

Method scope and applicability must include: the measurement process components validated, the nature of the analytes and matrices studied, the range of analyte levels, a description of known limitations/assumptions, and a description of how parameters meet DQOs/MQOs.
The standard deviation, and especially the repeatability standard deviation, should not be significantly larger than the method uncertainty.
Any observed bias, if statistically significant, should be smaller than the method uncertainty.

Use multiple analytical procedures (e.g., sample preparation, wet-chemistry, and/or instrumental analysis procedures) during an intralaboratory validation study to substantiate the specificity of the method being evaluated.
The fractional factorial experimental design is not intended for method optimization purposes.
